questions.question: Explain the concept of point processing in digital image processing. How does it differ from neighborhood processing?
keyPoints: Point processing transforms pixel values based only on individual pixel intensity
Output at (x,y) depends solely on input at same coordinate: s = T(r)
Differs from neighborhood processing which considers neighboring pixels for output
Used for intensity transformations, contrast enhancement, image negatives

questions.question: Derive the formula for image negative and explain its applications.
keyPoints: Formula: s = L - 1 - r for L gray levels, typically s = 255 - r
Inverts all intensity values: dark becomes bright, bright becomes dark
Useful for enhancing white or gray details in dark backgrounds
Used in medical imaging (X-rays) and as preprocessing step

questions.question: Explain log transformation with its formula. Why is it also called compression transformation?
keyPoints: Formula: s = c × log(1 + r), where c is constant
Compresses dynamic range of images with large intensity variations
Also called compression because log grows slowly, reducing large input variations
Useful for displaying Fourier spectra and images with high dynamic range

questions.question: Describe power-law (gamma) transformation. How does the value of γ affect image contrast?
keyPoints: Formula: s = c × r^γ
γ < 1: Expands dark regions (brighter image), compresses bright regions
γ > 1: Compresses dark regions (darker image), expands bright regions
Used for gamma correction in displays and contrast enhancement

questions.question: What is bit-plane slicing? Explain with an example for an 8-bit image.
keyPoints: Separates image into 8 binary images based on bit positions
Bit plane 7 (MSB) contains most significant visual information
Bit plane 0 (LSB) contains finest details
Used for image compression, encryption, and analyzing bit contributions

questions.question: Explain histogram equalization. How does it improve image contrast?
keyPoints: Redistributes gray levels to achieve uniform histogram
Uses cumulative distribution function (CDF) for mapping
Enhances contrast by utilizing full intensity range
Formula: s_k = (L-1) × Σ p_r(r_j) for all r_j ≤ r_k

questions.question: Differentiate between histogram equalization and histogram specification.
keyPoints: Equalization produces uniform histogram automatically
Specification matches a user-specified histogram shape
Specification gives more control over output distribution
Equalization is special case of specification with uniform target

questions.question: Explain contrast stretching (piecewise linear transformation). With a suitable diagram, describe its types and how it enhances low-contrast images.
keyPoints: Contrast stretching expands narrow intensity range to full display range
Types: Linear, thresholding (two-level), smooth transitions
Enhances image by stretching contrast in regions of interest
Can compress background while enhancing foreground
Formula involves defining input-output points for different intensity ranges
Effectiveness depends on choosing appropriate break points

questions.question: Derive and explain the mathematical formulation of histogram equalization. How does it achieve contrast enhancement?
keyPoints: Uses probability distribution: p_r(r) = n_k / n for each gray level
Cumulative distribution: s_k = T(r_k) = Σ p_r(r_j) for j=0 to k
For L levels: s_k = (L-1) × Σ p_r(r_j)
Achieves uniform distribution by mapping through CDF
Enhances contrast by utilizing full dynamic range
Output histogram approximates flat distribution

questions.question: Discuss intensity level slicing and its applications in image enhancement. How does it differ from bit-plane slicing?
keyPoints: Intensity level slicing highlights specific gray-level ranges
Sets pixels in range to bright value, others to dark (or vice versa)
Applications: medical imaging (bone detection), remote sensing (water bodies)
Differs from bit-plane slicing: operates on gray levels, not individual bits
Can use single range or multiple ranges with different display values
Useful for feature extraction and object detection

questions.question: Explain the various point processing transformation functions with their mathematical formulations. Give practical applications of each.
keyPoints: Negative: s = L-1-r, for X-ray enhancement
Log: s = c×log(1+r), for Fourier spectrum display
Power-law: s = c×r^γ, for gamma correction
Contrast stretching: piecewise linear for contrast enhancement
Bit-plane slicing: extracts individual bit contributions
Intensity slicing: highlights specific gray ranges
Histogram equalization: automatic contrast enhancement
